title: How Skilled Are Security Analysts?
abstract: The majority of security analysts are identified as skilled when the cross-section of analyst performance is modeled as a mixture of multiple skill distributions. Analysts exhibit heterogeneous skill—some are high-type, and some are low-type. On average, the recommendation revisions of both types exhibit positive abnormal returns. The heterogeneity stems from differential ability to produce new information; all analysts can profitably process news. Top analysts outperform because more of their recommendations are influential (i.e., associated with statistically significant returns) and both their influential and noninfluential recommendations are more informative. A majority of research firms are also identified as skilled.

article.introduction:
A fundamental question in financialeconomics is whether financial professionals are able to systematically identify misvalued securities. This question is important not only for understanding the nature of market efficiency, but also for understanding the role and value of financial intermediaries. A large body of research speaks to this question by examining the stock recommendations of security analysts. Overall, this literature finds that analyst recommendations and revisions to recommendations contain information about future returns (e.g., Stickel (1995), Womack (1996), Barber et al. (2001), Barber, Lehavy, and Trueman (2010)), although whether analysts produce new information or merely interpret contemporaneous news is subject to debate (Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009), Altınkılıç et al. (2013), Bradley et al. (2014), Li et al. (2015)).
Missing from prior literature, however, is evidence on how widespread such skill is across analysts. The fact that recommendations contain information on average does not necessarily mean that the median analyst is skilled, and there is little evidence concerning the value of the best and worst analysts. Whether analyst skill concentrates in a small subset of analysts or is ubiquitous in the industry speaks to the efficiency of markets as well as to the value of equity research for market participants. The value of analysts depends on both the extent of their skill and the nature of their informational ability (i.e., information processing versus information production). Such valuations are particularly important in light of recent regulation, namely, MiFID II in Europe (effective January 2018), which requires institutional investors to pay directly for equity research.
In this paper, we estimate the fraction of equity analysts who are skilled or unskilled at making stock recommendations. We also estimate the distribution of the economic magnitude of analyst skill. Identifying skilled analysts is challenging. Even in a world in which analysts make recommendations randomly, we would expect some variation in measured ability simply due to chance. A given analyst's recommendations may outperform due to good luck rather than superior information. Similarly, skilled analysts may underperform due to bad luck rather than true inability. To determine the fraction of skilled analysts, accounting for noise due to luck, we model analyst performance as a mixture distribution, using methods similar to those employed in recent studies on mutual funds (Harvey and Liu (2018)) and hedge funds (Chen, Cliff, and Zhao (2017)). These studies find that 10.6% of mutual funds and 66.6% of hedge funds are skilled. In our implementation, analysts belong to one of two skill groups. The abnormal performance of each of these skill groups is centered at a different level of abnormal returns, which is also estimated.1Our estimation uses information from the cross-section of analyst performance to reduce the noise inherent in estimating analyst-level performance.
Our primary result is striking. A large fraction of the almost 5,500 equity analysts studied between 1993 and 2015 appear skilled. When an analyst's performance is measured using the average abnormal return associated with their recommendation revisions, the mixture model indicates that 97% of analysts have positive true abnormal returns. This estimate is higher than the fraction of analysts with positive point estimates of performance (85%), so the use of cross-sectional information in assessing performance implies that some analysts with negative measured performance are unlucky rather than unskilled. The average magnitude of analyst skill is also large, with an abnormal return of 1.5% over a [0,5] trading-day window. Roughly 64% of analysts are estimated to come from a lower return distribution, but the average performance of these analysts is still economically large and positive, with the distribution centered at a 1% abnormal return. The remaining 36% of analysts perform even better, with abnormal performance centered at 2.4%.
We further find that analyst skill is persistent. In particular, the autoregressive coefficient from a regression of analyst performance measured over the second half of the sample on the performance measured in the first half is 0.24.2This estimate from the data is statistically indistinguishable from the persistence of 0.27 implied by the structural assumptions and estimates of the mixture model. These results suggest that the parametric assumptions underlying the mixture model do a good job characterizing the skill distribution.
Given how well analysts perform, an obvious question that arises relates to the source of this performance. In particular, does their performance stem from an ability to forecast the pricing implications of concurrent news events, from an ability to provide incremental information to the market, or both? Recent research by Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009) and Altınkılıç et al. (2013) concludes that recommendations are not informative beyond other concurrent information releases, but this conclusion is refuted by Bradley et al. (2014) and Li et al. (2015), who provide evidence that analyst picks do have incremental information content.3We contribute to this debate by testing whether skill at the analyst level is driven by “piggybacking” on news events (i.e., information processing), the production of new information, or both. Using the RavenPack news database to separately estimate analyst skill from revisions made with and without concurrent news releases, we find evidence of skill on both dimensions. First, all analysts appear to be skilled at forecasting drift associated with news events. The economic magnitude of this skill is large (2%), and the only variation across analysts on this dimension is due to estimation error. In addition, a sizable fraction of analysts (93%) that make revisions on nonnews days are skilled at information production as well. While the average magnitude of information production ability is still large (1.7%), there is considerable cross-sectional heterogeneity in analyst ability to produce new information—the cross-sectional standard deviation is 1.2%. Thus, the variation in overall analyst skill is driven by differences in information production ability.
While our estimates suggest that almost all analysts are skilled, there is substantial cross-sectional variation in the extent of that skill. Accordingly, we next examine the analyst characteristics that are associated with higher levels of skill. Specifically, we model the probability that a given analyst is drawn from the higher mean distribution as a function of the number of recommendations the analyst makes, the percentage of their picks that are sells, the concentration of the analyst's industry coverage, and the analyst's years of experience. Not surprisingly, we find that more experienced analysts are more likely to be drawn from the higher mean distribution, while those that make more recommendations per year are more likely to be low-type, consistent with diseconomies of scale. We further find that analysts covering more industries are more likely to be identified as high-type analysts, which is inconsistent with the view that more focused analysts perform better. Analysts issuing a larger fraction of sell recommendations are significantly more likely to come from the higher mean distribution, which is consistent with Barber et al. (2006) and suggests that conflicts of interest may play a role in the performance (Michaely and Womack (1999)).
The results above imply that analysts are important information intermediaries who help facilitate price discovery in the securities they cover. We next characterize the economic value of this information by estimating its dollar value. If the most skilled analysts make recommendations disproportionately in small stocks, the contribution of their information to market efficiency in dollar terms may be small even if the returns associated with their picks are large.4Indeed, we document a negative correlation between each analyst's expected abnormal return (based on the mixture model estimates) and the size of the companies they make recommendations on. We therefore convert their expected skill to dollars using analyst-specific measures of their underlying stocks' market capitalization and total institutional ownership. The median analyst's revisions are worth $22 million in terms of total market capitalization price discovery and $15 million in terms of total price discovery to institutional owners. While this is an estimate of the aggregate value a revision provides to the market, the entire firm is, of course, not tradeable at that price. We therefore also estimate the dollar value of a pick based on an average institutional position change in that stock. Based on this measure, we find that the median analyst's pick is worth approximately $40,000 to a given institutional client.
The skill we identify at the analyst level could be driven by analysts consistently making informative recommendations or could be due to all analysts having just a few highly profitable recommendations. To shed light on this question, we estimate a mixture model at the underlying recommendation level. As in the analyst-level analysis, this estimation reduces the noise in evaluating stock-level recommendations, which are even noisier than analyst-level performance measures. This approach also allows us to reconcile our analyst-level findings with the results of Loh and Stulz (2011), who find that only 12% of recommendation revisions are influential (i.e., statistically significantly affect stock prices) and that a full quarter of analysts never issue a single influential revision.
The recommendation-level mixture model indicates that 86% of revisions are informative (i.e., have positive true abnormal returns). Many of these informative recommendations are not classified as influential since individual stock returns are noisy. While we find that influential recommendations are quite good (the median noise-reduced conditional expected return is 6%), noninfluential recommendations are also informative, with a median conditional expected return of 1%. Ignoring these informative but noninfluential recommendations (which are by far more prevalent) results in understating the extent of ability in the cross-section of analysts. When we compare influential and noninfluential recommendations across terciles of expected skill based on the analyst-level mixture model, we find that the top-tercile analysts outperform not only because more of their recommendations are influential, but also because both their influential recommendations and their noninfluential recommendations have higher abnormal returns.
Finally, given the cross-sectional variation in analyst ability that we document, it is natural to examine performance at the brokerage level. Analysts drawn from the higher mean distribution may concentrate in a few brokerages due to better training, recruiting, or access to information. Moreover, research firms may face different incentives due to their other lines of business; these varying incentives may impact performance. When we model the cross-section of brokerages, we find that a large fraction of brokers are skilled, but the proportion is smaller than at the analyst level, consistent with some concentration of talent. A larger fraction of brokerages are drawn from the high-type distribution following the SEC's Global Research Analyst Settlement, consistent with Barber, Lehavy, and Trueman (2007). Larger brokerages, as measured by number of revisions, analysts, or industries covered, are more likely to be high-type brokerages.
Our results contribute to a better overall understanding of analyst skill. A long line of research investigates the output quality of equity analysts along the dimensions of recommendations (e.g., Stickel (1995), Womack (1996), Barber et al. (2001,2006), Barber, Lehavy, and Trueman (2010)), earnings forecasts (e.g., Stickel (1992)), and price targets (e.g., Brav and Lehavy (2003)). A number of these studies interpret analyst performance as skilled. For instance, Mikhail, Walther, and Willis (2004) find that relative analyst recommendation performance is persistent, consistent with skill. However, other work suggests that analyst recommendations may not be informative. For instance, Drake, Rees, and Swanson (2011) find that a strategy that trades with short-sellers andagainstanalyst recommendations is profitable. We also contribute to the debate on the information content of recommendations around news events, which is studied at the recommendation level by Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009), Altınkılıç et al. (2013), Bradley et al. (2014), and Li et al. (2015).
The result that a majority of analysts are skilled stands in stark contrast to skill-and-luck studies in the active mutual fund space. Kosowski et al. (2006), Barras, Scaillet, and Wermers (2010), Fama and French (2010), Crane and Crotty (2018), and Harvey and Liu (2018) find that, at best, a small minority of managers are able to outperform the market.5One important distinction between analysts and fund managers is that the former do not have to deploy capital following their stock picks, while the latter do not have this flexibility. It is likely the case that transaction costs would dramatically erode the profitability of trading on analyst recommendations. Evidence to this effect can be found in Barber et al. (2001) and Mikhail, Walther, and Willis (2004). Moreover, analysts do not have to issue recommendations, while fund managers must invest capital. Therefore, mutual funds' observed stock selection is more likely to be subject to diminishing returns to scale (Berk and Green (2004)) compared to analysts' stock recommendations.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the mixture modeling approach used in our analysis. SectionIIdescribes the sample and the performance measure for recommendation revisions. SectionIIIpresents the empirical results for analyst ability. In SectionIV, we model the distribution of individual recommendation revisions and reconcile our approach to previous work at the recommendation level. SectionVanalyzes performance at the brokerage level. Finally, SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Modeling Performance as a Mixture Dis:
We model the distribution of analyst skill as a mixture distribution of multiple skill groups. This approach has recently been applied to hedge fund performance (Chen, Cliff, and Zhao (2017)) and mutual fund performance (Harvey and Liu (2018)).6Analysts are assumed to belong toJskill groups. A fractionπjof analysts belong to skill groupj. Abnormal returns for skill groupjare centered atμj. The dispersion in the estimated abnormal return across analysts belonging to groupjis driven by two factors: true variation in ability and estimation error. Denote the true skill of analystibyαiand their estimated abnormal performance byα̂i. The true skill of analystibelonging to groupjis given byαi=μj+ωi, whereωiis a zero-mean normal random variable with varianceσj2. The estimated abnormal performance is measured with estimation error,ei, which is assumed to be independent ofωiand normally distributed around zero with a standard deviation ofsi, the standard error of analysti's abnormal performance. Thus, the estimated abnormal performance of analystiis given byα̂i=μj+ωi+ei. In our empirical work, we find that a mixture ofJ=2skill groups best fits the cross-section of analyst performance, so our discussion proceeds assumingJ=2.
Figure1illustrates the mixture method graphically for the two-distribution case. The top panel shows the distribution of true skill. Analysts in the dashed blue distribution (Component 1) are more skilled (on average) than those in the solid red distribution (μ0<μ1). Dispersion in the conditional distributions is due toσj. The bottom panel plots the distribution of estimated ability. Because performance is measured with noise, the conditional dispersion in observed ability is greater. In the figure, the standard error is assumed to be constant across analysts(si=s∀i), so that the dispersion in each conditional distribution isσij=σj2+s2.
We estimate the parameters both with and without the restrictions thatμ0=0andσ0=0. Under these restrictions, all analysts from the first distribution have true abnormal returns of exactly zero, and thus, variation in their estimated performance is entirely due to luck (i.e., the realization ofei). In this case, the estimate of π0represents the fraction of zero-skill analysts, as opposed to analysts with skill (αi>0) or negative true ability (αi<0). This approach is analogous to recent work in the mutual fund performance literature (Barras, Scaillet, and Wermers (2010)) that estimates proportions of skilled and unskilled funds using false discovery rate (FDR) methods.7It is possible, however, that a homogeneous group of zero-skill analysts does not exist. We can explicitly test if this is the case by relaxing the above restrictions. The unconstrained model allows us to more flexibly model heterogeneity in skill in the cross-section of analysts. Absent the restrictions thatμ0=0andσ0=0, the estimate of π0no longer represents the fraction of zero-skill analysts. In either case, we estimate the fraction of skilled analysts by calculating the proportion of the mixture distribution with positive true ability,αi>0.
article.II. Data:
The data on analyst stock recommendations come from Thomson Reuters's Institutional Brokers Estimates (I/B/E/S) database and cover the period 1993 to 2015. We merge individual analyst stock recommendations with return information from CRSP and NYSE TAQ.
As discussed in the Introduction, the timing of calculating abnormal returns for analyst recommendations is subject to debate (Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009), Altınkılıç et al. (2013), Bradley et al. (2014), Li et al. (2015)). In our sample, some recommendations occur during trading hours, while others occur before or after trading hours. For after-hours recommendations (17.6% of the sample), the day 0 return is the CRSP return for the day following the recommendation. For before-hours recommendations (38.4% of the sample), the day 0 return is the CRSP return for the day on which the recommendation is made.12For intraday recommendations (44% of the sample), we obtain the prevailing quote midpoint from NYSE TAQ for stockjand SPY as of the I/B/E/S time stamp and calculate the day 0 return from that quote midpoint to the closing price.13The benchmark return on day 0 is the corresponding intraday return for SPY. Bradley et al. (2014) find that intraday I/B/E/S time stamps are often later than those from newswire sources. Since they find that prices generally move in the direction of the recommendation, the use of the I/B/E/S time stamp is conservative with respect to our tests of analyst skill. Using intraday returns should also mitigate the concern that we are capturing price impact due only to the news announcement rather than price movement following a subsequent revision. We investigate the relation between performance and news in detail in SectionIII.C.
We plot the distributions of abnormal returns,ABRik, for individual revisions in Figure2. The figure presents histograms for upgrades (Panel A) and downgrades (Panel B) based on an analyst's change in recommendation. The average (signed) abnormal return is positive following both upgrades and downgrades. Overall, these patterns at the recommendation level are consistent with prior literature (e.g., Loh and Stulz (2011)).
To be included in the sample, we require that an analyst has at least 20 recommendation revisions.15We omit analysts with the I/B/E/S unique identifier “amaskcd” of zero since these analysts are not identified separately in the I/B/E/S data. The sample for average returns following recommendations contains 5,478 analysts responsible for 356,077 total recommendation revisions. TableIreports cross-sectional statistics of analyst-specific average abnormal returns for recommendation revisions. Figure3plots the distribution of these returns. The cross-sectional average (50thpercentile)ABR¯iis 171 (151) bps. There is substantial cross-sectional variation in analyst average abnormal returns (the standard deviation is 192 bps), and the distribution is skewed and exhibits excess kurtosis, consistent with a possible mixture distribution in the underlying skill distribution. Indeed, we can reject the null hypothesis (at the 1% level) that the distribution of average abnormal returns is normally distributed. While the majority of average abnormal returns are positive, only 42% of analysts have significantly positive average abnormal returns at a 5% one-sided test size. Some of the analysts with significant abnormal returns may just be lucky, while some of the analysts with insignificant average abnormal returns may be skilled but unlucky.
It is worth noting that while the cross-sectional average level of skill seems economically large, these analyst-specific averages are measured with substantial noise. The cross-sectional average (median) standard error is 127 (108) bps. As a result, the average (median) analyst'st-statistic is only 1.44 (1.41). We also find substantial cross-sectional variation in the standard errors—the distribution of standard errors has a cross-sectional standard deviation of 71 bps. We explicitly account for this cross-sectional variation in estimation error when characterizing the cross-section of skill in SectionIII.16
article.III. The Cross-Section of Analyst Skill:
We first estimate a constrained two-component mixture model in which all analysts in the lower skill component are constrained to have no abnormal performance (i.e.,μ0=0andσ0=0). TableIIreports the estimates for the fraction of analysts in each of the two distributions (π0,π1), as well as the location and dispersion of the second distribution (μ1and σ1) and the dispersion in average estimated ability (σij) for each distribution. The estimated proportion of analysts coming from the low-type distribution (centered atμ0=0) is only 5.6%. All of the variation for these zero-skill analysts is assumed to be due to estimation error. The estimation indicates that 94.4% of analysts are drawn from a distribution centered at 1.58% abnormal return.17Therefore, not only is the mass of high-type analysts large, but also the skill distribution is centered at an economically large positive return. This distribution has a standard deviation of 98 bps, so some of the analysts in the left tail of this distribution are estimated to have negative true ability. The overall fraction of unskilled analysts consists of zero-skill analysts (Component 0) and analysts from Component 1 with negative true ability. The model estimates the former to beπ0=0.0556and the latter to be(1−π0)Φ(0−μ1σ1)=0.9444·Φ(−0.01580.0098)=0.0505, whereΦ(·)denotes the normal distribution function, so the total fraction of unskilled analysts is 10.6%. The constrained mixture model approach in TableIItherefore indicates that the majority of analysts (89.4%) are skilled.
In the Appendix, we examine the robustness of this constrained estimation to the parametric assumption that high-type analyst ability is normally distributed, which has the counterintuitive feature that some high-type analysts have negative true ability. Michaely and Womack (1999) and Agrawal and Chen (2008) find that conflicts of interest may result in biased stock recommendations, so it is reasonable to allow true skill to be negative for some analysts. However, labor market competition may be sufficient to drive such analysts out of the market quickly, in which case no analyst would exhibit true negative performance. It is therefore important to check the sensitivity of our results to the assumption of normality. To do so, we restrict high-type analysts' true ability to be positive by modeling skilled analysts as drawn from a lognormal distribution, where each analyst's performance is still measured with normally distributed error. TableAIshows that the fraction of skilled analysts under these assumptions is 88.4%, which is remarkably close to the estimate from TableIIof 89.4%.
While the constrained models provide initial evidence that most analysts are skilled, they may needlessly restrict the models' ability to capture heterogeneity in analyst skill. It is possible that while almost all analysts are skilled, some are better than others (i.e., the first-component distribution is centered to the right of zero and has dispersion). To address this possibility, we estimate unconstrained mixture models in which each (normal) component distribution can have a different location parameterμj, not necessarily equal to zero, and each distribution can exhibit dispersion in true skill. Both the adjusted BIC and the likelihood ratio test described in SectionI.Areject the constrained two-component model from TableIIin favor of an unconstrained model.
We estimate unconstrained mixture models for one-, two-, three-, and four-component distributions and select the best model using the adjusted BIC and the bootstrapped likelihood ratio test. Both methods indicate that a two-component distribution best fits the data. The adjusted BIC values for the one-, two-, three-, and four-component distributions are −29,445.86, −29,553.21, −29,542.68, and −29,527.49, respectively. Similarly, the likelihood ratio tests between the one- and two-component models reject the null of a one-component model in favor of the two-component model in all of the bootstrap runs, but we fail to reject the two-component model in favor of the three-component model at the 1% significance level.18
The estimates for the unconstrained two-component mixture distribution are tabulated in TableIII. We bootstrap the standard errors of the estimates by sampling (with replacement) 100 times from the underlying data and estimating the various mixture model statistics for each bootstrap sample. We report the standard deviation of the resulting distribution in each of our tables.
A majority of analysts (64%) fall in the lower mean component distribution. Most of these low-type analysts are skilled—the distribution is centered at a mean of 0.99%. The remaining 36% of analysts fall in the high-type distribution centered at a mean of 2.44%.19The underlying skill for the second component is more variable, however, with the dispersion in skill equal to 1.13% compared to 0.56% for the first component.
Panel B of TableIIIreports statistics of the model's estimate of the cross-sectional distribution of analyst skill.20The model estimates average true skill to be 1.51% and median analyst skill to be 1.27%. Importantly, the vast majority of analysts are estimated to have positive skill—the fraction of analysts with nonnegative skill is almost 97%. Note that this is substantially higher than the proportion of analysts with positive average returns following revisions reported in TableI(85%). Also, recall from TableIthat only 53% of analysts have statistically significant positive performance at the 10% level. Thus, if one were to examine how many analysts are skilled using traditional hypothesis testing independently for each analyst, many analysts identified as skilled by the mixture model would be incorrectly classified as unskilled. Even the 10thpercentile of the estimated analyst distribution is significantly positive at a return of 0.38%. Information from the cross-section of analyst performance therefore implies that the negative measured abnormal performance for some analysts is a result of bad luck rather than true negative ability.
We compare this empirical persistence estimate to that implied by the structure imposed by the mixture model in two ways. First, we evaluate equation (8) using estimates ofvar(ωi)andvar(ei,t). The parameters for the unconstrained mixture model estimated using the 1993 to 2004 subsample areΘ̂={π̂0,μ̂0,μ̂1,σ̂0,σ̂1}={0.8519,0.0072,0.0217,0.0049,0.0101}.22The resulting variance of the true ability,var(ωi), implied by the mixture model is 0.00802. If we evaluate equation (8) using this value and the average standard error for analysts entering regression (9) of 0.0132, the impliedρ̂is 0.27. This model-implied estimate is very close to the empirical estimate from the data; the model-implied estimate falls within a standard confidence interval of the regression estimate in (9).
This first method ignores the heteroskedastic nature of the errors,ei,t, in measured performanceABR¯i,t. To incorporate this feature of the data, we simulate the distribution ofρ̂implied by the estimated mixture model and the empirical distribution ofsi. For each simulation run, we draw a sample ofωifrom the estimated mixture distribution and also draw estimation errorsei,tfor periods 1993 to 2004 and 2005 to 2015 from the empirical distribution ofsifor the 1,028 analysts making at least 20 recommendations in both periods. We use the resulting two-period panel to estimate theρ̂implied by the structure assumed by the mixture model. We repeat this simulation procedure 5,000 times to obtain a distribution ofρ̂implied by the model. This distribution is plotted in Figure4. Remarkably, the empirical estimate ofρ̂=0.24falls in the center of the simulated parameter distribution. Both methods suggest that the unconstrained normal mixture model is not misspecified, but rather does a good job characterizing the skill distribution in terms of matching the observed performance persistence.
In this section, we explore the economic channel underlying analysts' ability. A number of studies argue that analysts largely issue recommendations that piggyback on other news events, and that recommendation revisions do not provide much incremental information to the market beyond that already embedded in the news. For instance, Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009) argue that analyst recommendations have little impact on prices once other corporate news is taken into account. Loh and Stulz (2011) further argue that including recommendations that are accompanied by earnings announcements will inflate analyst performance. On the other hand, Li et al. (2015) show that many revisions are actually contrarian relative to the prevailing price movements in the underlying stocks and that revisions issued in the absence of news events also contribute to price discovery.
The ubiquity of skill that we document in the overall analyst cross-section could be due to analysts forecasting drift following news events. Alternatively, it could be due to analysts producing novel information. We refer to the former channel as information processing and the latter as information production. While both channels are potentially valuable to market participants, they have different implications for understanding the role that analysts play in the market. It is also possible that analysts are successful on both accounts, but that the magnitude or cross-sectional variation in analyst ability could differ substantially across these two different economic channels.
To study the source of analyst skill, we first obtain a comprehensive list of news events from RavenPack News Analytics. Lett=0denote the time of the recommendation in the I/B/E/S database. We classify recommendations that follow within three days of a news event (specifically, recommendations with news events in the time window [ − 72 hours, −20 minutes]) for the underlying stock in this news database as information processing revisions and other recommendations as information production revisions. The RavenPack data begin in 2000, so this analysis runs from 2000 to 2015. The information processing revisions are those following any news event or press release with a RavenPack relevance score over 75 except those that are related to the analyst themselves (upgrade/downgrade announcements) as well as announcements about trading issues in the stock or the macroeconomy (specifically, group names of order-imbalances, stock-prices, technical-analysis, balance-of-payments, civil-unrest, government). The information processing categorization thus captures a large number of corporate events.23
We next evaluate an analyst's information processing ability by calculating their average performance across news-following revisions, and their information production ability by calculating the average performance across nonnews-following revisions.24Note that for information processing revisions, the use of intraday returns gives an analyst credit for any price drift following their recommendation, but not for any price movement due to the news event that occurs prior to the analyst recommendation. Analysts telling clients to buy is valuable if the price subsequently rises regardless of whether that price movement is due to the analyst's recommendation or the accompanying news event.
Panels A and B of TableIVreport mixture model estimates for analysts based on the information processing (i.e., news-following) and information production (non-event) samples, respectively. For the news subsample, the best-fitting model is not a mixture of two-component distributions. Rather, the estimation indicates that information processing ability is drawn from a single distribution in which all of the variation in observed performance for picks following news events is due to estimation error.25Thus, the model indicates that all analysts are quite good at piggybacking on news; the estimated true ability for these analysts is about 2%.
Are analysts also able to produce new information in the absence of contemporaneous news? The answer is yes. The mixture estimates in Panel B indicate that 93% of analysts who make revisions following nonnews days are also skilled at producing novel information.26However, the results also show that the cross-sectional variation in true skill overall is driven by these types of recommendations. In the no-event sample, most analysts (97%) are drawn from a skill distribution with a mean of 1.65% and a cross-sectional standard deviation of 1.12%. The remaining high-type analysts have average information production ability of 2.8%. In the full cross-section, average information production ability is 1.68%, which is lower than the information processing ability estimated in Panel A. Analyst information production ability exhibits a substantial amount of variation, unlike information processing ability. This is intuitive—it is relatively straightforward for market participants following a given firm to understand the implications of news, but it is harder to produce new information.
Another way to examine whether analyst skill is driven solely by forecasting drift (i.e., piggybacking) is to condition on returns immediately preceding a revision. Such analysis has been conducted in prior work at the recommendation level to rule out the possibility that a given revision is merely the result of piggybacking on news. Specifically, Altınkılıç and Hansen (2009) and Li et al. (2015) consider recommendations that are contrarian, in the sense that the information content of the revision does not correspond to the most recent price movement in the stock.27TableIA.XIof the Internet Appendix reports estimates for analyst skill based on contrarian versus trending revision subsamples. The results are largely consistent with those presented in TableIV.28Using the trending revisions subsample, a larger overall fraction of analysts are skilled, with lower cross-sectional variation in true skill, similar to the news sample. Nonetheless, most analysts are still found to be skilled when making contrarian picks, with the magnitude of that skill economically significant.
It is likely harder for analysts with greater workloads to consistently provide recommendations with high abnormal performance. Consistent with this view, analysts issuing a larger number of recommendations per year are less likely to be drawn from the high-type distribution—36% of analysts with above-median workloads are drawn from the high-type distribution, while almost 60% of analysts with below-median workloads are identified as high-type analysts. The difference is statistically significant.29The mean estimated return is 31 bps lower for analysts with higher workloads. However, the estimated fraction of skilled analysts is about 97% for both sets of analysts.
Analysts with a larger fraction of sell recommendations are more likely to be high-type analysts. Specifically, the model estimates that 57% of analysts with above-median rates of sell recommendations are high-type, compared to only 39% of analysts with lower rates of sell recommendations. The conditional average (median) return of the less favorable analysts is 1.62% (1.36%), which is 25 bps higher than the same estimate for the more favorable analysts. These findings corroborate those of Barber et al. (2006), who provide portfolio-based evidence that recommendations of less favorable analysts outperform those of more favorable analysts.
Analysts who cover more industries are also more likely to be high-type analysts. Over half of the analysts with above-median industry coverage (based on the number of unique four-digit SIC codes represented in an analyst's recommendations) are high-type analysts, while only a third of analysts with more focused industry coverage are high-type. This is likely due to more skilled analysts being asked to cover more industries.
Mikhail, Walther, and Willis (1997) find that analyst forecast errors decrease with firm-specific experience, but recommendations by more experienced analysts are not more profitable than those of less experienced analysts. In our sample, overall analyst tenure is strongly associated with the probability of being a high-type analyst—87% of analysts with above-median tenure are high-type, while only 39% of newer analysts are identified as drawn from the higher mean component distribution. The difference in the average analyst skill between newer and older analysts is almost 70 bps. The difference in the median skill between the two groups is even larger, at 106 bps. Despite these differences, the vast majority of both groups are identified as skilled (at about 98%), although the economic content of revisions made by less experienced analysts is lower.
The level and extent of skill may change over time. For instance, the introduction of regulations that are directly related to analysts' ability to access information (e.g., Reg FD and the SEC's Global Research Analyst Settlement) could have impacted analyst performance. Moreover, industry consolidation could also have altered analysts' incentives or changed the composition of analysts. We therefore examine the level of skill over our sample period. We first calculateABR¯iover rolling 10-year periods starting with the period 1993 to 2002 and ending with the period 2006 to 2015. We then estimate a two-component mixture distribution over each 10-year rolling window.
We plot the period-by-period results in Figure5. Panel A presents the time series of the estimated cross-sectional mean as well as the first and third quantiles. We plot each point as of the last year in the rolling period. As can be seen, the economic magnitude of analyst skill increases over the first half of the sample. For instance, the mean analyst return rises from about 75 bps for the period ending 2002 to over 2% by the second half of the sample. Both the first and third quartiles also increase. The increase is greater for the third quartile, so the cross-section of estimated ability becomes more dispersed over time. Panel B shows that the vast majority of analysts are skilled (i.e., have positive ability) regardless of the subsample, and the fraction of skilled analysts also increases over time. Overall, the evidence suggests that analysts are getting better over time. In SectionV, we examine time-series variation in brokerage skill by breaking the sample into subperiods based on the SEC's Global Research Analyst Settlement. The results are consistent with those above and suggest that improved incentives may be related to better performance over time. We also note that analyst tenure is greater in the later part of the sample (the correlation between analyst tenure within each 10-year period and the estimated fraction of skilled analysts is 0.72), suggesting that some of the improvement may be due to consolidation in the industry resulting in more experienced analysts on average.
The previous analyses imply that analysts are important information intermediaries that help facilitate price discovery in the securities they cover. One way to characterize the economic value of this information is by expressing the value in dollar terms. If the most skilled analysts we identify using return measures facilitate price discovery only in microcap stocks, it may be the case that the analysts we estimate to be less skilled (i.e., lower expected return per recommendation) actually facilitate greater price discovery in dollar terms. For instance, in the mutual fund literature, Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) argue that performance should be measured in dollar terms since fund managers who earn smaller returns on extremely large AUM generate more value than managers with larger returns, but small AUM. Accordingly, for each analyst, we calculate their expected skill in returns based on the mixture model and convert this into dollar terms using analyst-specific measures of the underlying stocks' market capitalization and aggregate institutional ownership. More specifically, we scale the expected return by the median market capitalization and institutional ownership across all stocks the analyst covers over the sample.
These calculations shed light on the dollar value of analysts in terms of price discovery for overall market capitalization and institutional positions. A distinct question is how much a single analyst client (i.e., a given institutional investor) might value a given analyst's recommendation in dollar terms. The answer to this question depends, of course, on the ability of investors to trade on the information. We estimate the magnitude of possible trades in the underlying securities using quarterly changes in institutional holdings from 13-F filings over our 1993 to 2015 sample period. For each stock on which a recommendation is made, we calculate the average (unsigned) change in institutional positions in that quarter (conditional on a nonzero position change). This provides us with a dollar-value estimate of how much each stock is traded by institutional investors on average in a given quarter.30We then take the median of this value across all stocks that are the subject of a given analyst's revisions as our analyst-specific measure of a typical institutional position change.
Panel A of TableVIreports the cross-sectional correlation of the conditional expected skill measure (equation (14)) for each analyst and the underlying stocks' characteristics in terms of market capitalization, aggregate institutional ownership, and magnitude of institutional ownership changes. The conditional expected skill measure is negatively correlated with both market capitalization and institutional ownership. These results support the view that analysts are able to produce information with higher returns for smaller firms. The question is whether the increases in return magnitudes offset earning those returns on a smaller base in dollar terms for the underlying firms. The trading measure is also negatively correlated with expected alpha, although the magnitude is slightly lower. To take these relations into account, we multiply each analyst's expected alpha by the median dollar value measure. More specifically, for each stock, we measure the average (deflated) dollar market capitalization, dollar institutional ownership, and dollar institutional trade size. Then, for each analyst, we take the median of these dollar estimates across all of the stocks the analyst makes recommendations on and we multiply it by the analyst's conditional expected return. These dollar measures of performance thus capture the cross-sectional relations between performance in return space and, for example, the size of the underlying stocks.
We tabulate the cross-sectional variation in performance under each of these dollar measures in Panel B of TableVI. The average conditional expectation of analyst skill is 1.51%. When accounting for the relation between the forecasts and firm size, the average price discovery due to analyst revisions is $42 million (column (2)). The median is $22 million. The difference reflects the skewed market capitalization distribution. If analyst recommendations are valuable primarily to institutional clients, it makes sense to measure the price discovery associated with the fraction of the firms owned by such institutions. We present these results in column (3) of Panel B. The average value of price discovery in terms of aggregate institutional ownership positions is $30 million.
Of course, one would not be able to purchase all of the outstanding shares in a company based on an upgrade or liquidate the aggregate institutional ownership following a downgrade in a short time horizon without extreme price impact. In column (4) of Panel B, we report estimates of the cross-sectional distribution of dollar values to analysts' institutional clients. If an institution were able to trade a typical quarterly holding change based on the recommendation, the value of a recommendation by the average (median) analyst would be $50,000 ($40,200).31Institutional clients subscribe to research rather than pay piecemeal, so the value of a subscription to a given client reflects the total number of revisions made by the analyst (or analysts). The average analyst in our sample makes 15 revisions per year, so the aggregated value of an analyst due to multiple recommendations is higher.
Finally, in Panel C of TableVI, we sort analysts into terciles based on the size of the firms on which they make recommendations (based on an analyst's median firm in terms of market capitalization). In the first column, we again observe a negative relation between expected skill and the size of the underlying firms. However, this drop in expected return is more than offset by differences in size across the terciles, that is, the analysts covering larger firms create more overall value.
article.IV. The Informativeness of Individual Re:
The preceding analysis focuses on how many analysts are skilled. In this section, we relate the analyst-level skill estimates to the informativeness of the underlying revisions. It is possible that most analysts make relatively few good recommendations, but the good recommendations have large abnormal returns. Alternatively, it is possible that most of the revisions are informative. We find evidence of the latter. Using a mixture model to reduce the noise inherent in the underlying stock returns, we find that the majority of revisions (86%) are informative. Our findings stand in contrast to those of Loh and Stulz (2011), who identify only 12% of revisions as influential (i.e., statistically significantly informative). In SectionIII.B, we relate their recommendation-level analysis to our identification of skilled analysts from SectionIII. At the analyst level, the mixture model uses information from both influential (in the Loh and Stulz (2011) sense) and noninfluential revisions to identify skilled analysts. While influential revisions are more informative, noninfluential revisions also have economically meaningful information content.
Panel A of TableVIIreports the recommendation-level distribution of abnormal returns following analyst revisions. For each revision, we calculate a stock-specific standard error by estimating a market model regression at the daily frequency over the [−272,−21] trading-day window relative to each revision. We scale the resulting estimate of the daily idiosyncratic volatility from the market model into idiosyncratic volatility corresponding to the [0,5] trading day window,sik. We find that the cross-sectional average abnormal return is 1.83%. The distribution is skewed—the median abnormal return is 1.13%, and a majority (60%) of the revisions are associated with positive abnormal returns. Stock-level returns are noisy—the cross-sectional average idiosyncratic volatility is 6.27%, and the averaget-statistic of the returns is only 0.32. At the 5% significance level, only 11% of recommendations are associated with statistically significant abnormal returns. This fraction is quite close to the estimate in Loh and Stulz (2011) that 12% of recommendations are influential.32
Based on the summary statistics above alone, it is not surprising that our analyst-level mixture model results in different conclusions about the extent of analyst skill. The noise in the individual recommendations is substantial. Analysis at the analyst level, which employs a portfolio approach, would be substantially less noisy than considering individual recommendations independently. Alternatively, one could account for noise in individual recommendations by applying the mixture model at therecommendationlevel. The latter approach is natural to consider as the Loh and Stulz (2011) results do not explicitly incorporate the multiple-hypotheses issues inherent in treating each recommendation as a separate test. Note also that it is clear from the summary statistics that the distribution of recommendations is not normally distributed. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test strongly rejects normality at the 1% level. Each of these issues can lead to incorrect inference at standard critical values.
We therefore estimate a recommendation-level mixture model, assuming that the informativeness of each revision is drawn fromJ-component distributions. The underlying informativeness in terms of abnormal returns for a given recommendation in componentjisμj+ωkfor recommendationk. The additional noise in the observed abnormal return is assumed to have zero mean and variance(sik)2modeled using the estimated idiosyncratic variance described above. We report estimates from a three-component mixture model in Panel B of TableVII.33Each of the three components is centered at a positive mean. The first component has a mean of 94 bps and contains 89% of the mass of the distribution. The second component accounts for another 10% of the mass. On average, the recommendations in this distribution are quite informative. The average abnormal return is 6.2%, and the dispersion is also high at 10.8%. The probability that a recommendation belongs to the third component is only 1%. These recommendations have even higher mean and variance—the mean is 13% and the variance is 47%.
Panel C of TableVIIreports summary statistics of the fitted recommendation revision distribution. The average (median) abnormal return is 1.57% (0.98%), and the interquartile range is about 1.2%. In addition, 86% of the revision distribution is estimated to be positive mean, which is substantially higher than the 60% of observed abnormal returns that are positive. This result suggests that a large fraction of recommendations are unlucky in the sense that the realized abnormal return was negative but the unobserved information content of the revision was positive. The estimate that 86% of the revisions are informative stands in stark contrast to the estimate from Loh and Stulz (2011) that only 12% of recommendations are influential. Clearly, the information content of many of the recommendations is swamped by the noise in the underlying stock returns. When we apply the mixture model at either the recommendation level or the analyst level, our conclusion holds: most analysts issue informative recommendations.
In this section, we reconcile two conflicting results from our analysis with the influential-recommendation approach of Loh and Stulz (2011). First, defining an influential recommendation as one with at(ABRik)of at least 1.96, they report that a small minority of revisions (12%) are influential. In our sample, this fraction is slightly smaller than 10%. How can we reconcile this estimate with our finding that 86% of recommendations are informative (e.g., have a positive expected return)? Second, our analyst-level results indicate that the vast majority of analysts are skilled, while Loh and Stulz (2011) conclude that a sizable fraction (25%) never issue an influential recommendation and almost no analyst's median recommendation is influential. How can we reconcile this result with our estimate that 97% of analysts are skilled? In TableVIII, we present several analyses to contrast our approach with that of Loh and Stulz (2011).
First, we compare the informativeness implied by the mixture model approach for influential and noninfluential recommendations. The overall frequency of informative recommendations as estimated by the mixture model in TableVIIis 86%, but fewer than 10% of recommendations are influential in the Loh and Stulz (2011) sense in our sample. This suggests that noninfluential recommendations are likely to have economically meaningful abnormal returns despite the fact that they are statistically noisy. This effect is masked, however, if one does not use information across all recommendations to reduce the noise. Panel A of TableVIIIreports means and medians of recommendation-level performance measures across all recommendations identified as influential or not as in Loh and Stulz (2011). For each recommendation, we calculate a noise-reduced recommendation-level conditional expected abnormal return,E[ABRik]. This is the three-distribution analog to equation (14) calculated using the recommendation-level mixture model estimates reported in TableVII. For influential recommendations, the average conditional expected abnormal return is over 10%. The averageE[ABRik]for noninfluential recommendations is 81 bps. While much lower, a return of 81 bps is still economically meaningful over the short horizon we study.
The difference across influential and noninfluential recommendations is even larger when we consider the average realized abnormal return. This reflects the noise-reduction function of the mixture model. By explicitly using information from the entire cross-section of recommendations rather than treating them independently, our model accounts for the fact that part of the lower returns to noninfluential revisions may be due to bad luck, while part of the higher returns to influential revisions may be due to good luck.
If we consider the median for eitherE[ABRik]or realizedABRik(across recommendations), the economic gap between influential and noninfluential recommendations is lower. The conditional expectation is 6% for influential recommendations and 1% for noninfluential recommendations. Thus, while influential recommendations are quite valuable, failing to also consider noninfluential recommendations ignores a great deal of economically informative, if noisy, recommendations. This ultimately results in a less favorable characterization when evaluating analysts.
Second, we examine how our analyst-level identification of skill relates to influential recommendations. In Panel B of TableVIII, we calculate performance statistics as a function of whether underlying recommendations are influential (or not) across terciles of our measure of conditional expected skill,E[αi], defined in equations (14) to (17). Across the three terciles,E[αi]ranges from 81 bps to 2.3%. We see variation inE[αi]for several reasons. First, the propensity of analysts to issue influential recommendations rises across the terciles. For the average analyst in the lowestE[αi]tercile, fewer than 4% of their recommendations are influential, while for the average analyst in the top tercile, 12% of their recommendations are influential in the Loh and Stulz (2011) sense. Second, the influential recommendations are economically more informative for the higher type analysts. Conditional on revisions being influential, the average realized abnormal return increases from 14% to 21% across the terciles. Third, the average realized abnormal return also differs across terciles for the noninfluential recommendations. These conditional averages range from −53 bps for the lowest tercile to 1.44% for the top tercile. Thus, the informativeness of noninfluential revisions by the top tercile is still economically meaningful despite the fact that these picks are in noisy stocks (indeed, the averaget-statistic for these revisions is only 0.21). When we calculateE[αi]for a given analyst, we do so based on the portfolio of their recommendations, so much of this noise diversifies away. Overall, top-tercile analysts outperform because more of their recommendations are influential, their influential recommendations have higher abnormal returns, and their noninfluential returns are also more informative.
article.V. Performance at the Brokerage Level:
In this section, we assess how pervasive skill is across brokerages by applying the mixture model to abnormal returns at the brokerage level. With the majority of analysts showing evidence of skill, we would expect to see a relatively high fraction of skill at the brokerage level. Nonetheless, given the heterogeneity of skill at the analyst level, examining estimates for brokers is informative as to the nature of the research industry. Are the best analysts concentrated in a subset of firms, or is talent more diffuse across brokers? To answer this question, we aggregate to the brokerage level using the average return across all revisions within a given brokerage, which we denote byABR¯broker. We calculate standard errors,sbroker, clustering by both time and analyst to account for multiple revisions made on the same day or by the same analyst. TableIXreports summary statistics for the brokerage cross-section (Panel A), the mixture model estimates (Panel B), and the resulting performance distribution (Panel C). The cross-sectional averageABR¯brokeris 1.32%. As with the analyst averages, the distribution is skewed—the median brokerage's average abnormal return is 1.13%. In addition, 86% of the brokerages exhibit a positive average recommendation revision abnormal return, with 69% of these significant at the 10% level.
The two-component mixture model indicates that the proportions of brokerages across the two distributions are relatively equal.34Moreover, both are centered at positive values of 100 and 165 bps, respectively. The resulting cross-sectional distribution of skill has a mean of 1.3%, and 89% of brokerages are estimated to be skilled. While still large, the estimates are slightly lower than the equivalent analyst-level estimates. These results are consistent with some concentration of skill in the better-performing brokerage houses.
In TableX, we allow the probabilities that a broker belongs to each component of the mixture to be a function of various characteristics, as we do for analysts in SectionIII.D. We also allow the probabilities to differ for the same brokerage before versus after the SEC's Global Research Analyst Settlement, which was an important event for analysts and brokerages. The Global Settlement had the potential to reduce conflicts of interest for some analysts and brokers by limiting their interaction with, for example, underwriting services at the same investment bank. At the same time, these restrictions may have reduced the flow of valuable information that analysts used to make recommendations. By allowing the probability that a given brokerage is drawn from the higher mean component distribution to be related to this event, we can explicitly test whether the Global Settlement impacted brokers.
The estimates, reported in the first two columns of TableX, indicate that a larger fraction of brokerages are high-type after the Global Settlement (64% after versus 56% before).35This result is consistent with the Global Settlement reducing the agency problems inherent to some of the research firms. It is also consistent with evidence in Barber, Lehavy, and Trueman (2007) that independent research firm buy recommendations outperform investment bank buy recommendations. While our prior results on the cross-section of analysts suggest that conflicts of interest over the full sample are not so large as to generate a significant proportion of “bad” analysts, the brokerage results suggest that such conflicts may nonetheless be driving down the information contained in recommendations.
In TableX, we also consider above-median indicators for various brokerage characteristics. In general, the differences in probability associated with the characteristics are noisier than when comparing across analyst characteristics. This is likely due to the fact that the broker sample is substantially smaller, so that each cross-sectional split contains a relatively small number of observations. Nonetheless, the economic magnitudes of some of the differences are large. For example, 80% of brokers making an above-median number of revisions are drawn from the higher mean distribution compared to 9% for the below-median brokers. This difference is significant at the 10% level. There is also evidence that brokers with a larger stable of analysts perform better. The mean and median of the fitted distribution are both higher at the 5% level. Combined with the result from SectionIII.Dthat busy analysts perform worse, this suggests that good brokers make more recommendations by hiring more staff rather than requiring that their analysts manage a larger workload.
Brokers that cover a large number of industries are drawn from the higher mean distribution, while those with more concentrated industry coverage are drawn from the low-mean distribution. The difference in means of the fitted distribution is large (44 bps) and statistically significant at the 10% level. Broker-level performance does not significantly differ across the other characteristics.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, we estimate how many security analysts are skilled. The results suggest that almost all analysts are skilled. Analysts exhibit heterogeneous skill—some pick stocks that perform much better on average than other analysts' picks. This variation is a function of analyst characteristics such as the number of recommendations the analyst makes, the percentage of the analyst's picks that are sells, the industry focus of the analyst, and the analyst's tenure. However, even analysts in the lower mean distribution exhibit economically meaningful, positive performance. We find that an average analyst's typical recommendation is worth about $50,000 to an institutional client. Importantly, analyst skill is not driven solely by revisions that piggyback on firm news. Rather, most analysts are also skilled at producing new information. Top analysts outperform because more of their recommendations are influential and both their influential and noninfluential recommendations are more informative than those of other analysts. Finally, at the broker level, both the proportion and the magnitude of skill are slightly lower than in the analyst distribution, consistent with some concentration of skilled analysts at the best brokers.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: Constrained Mixture Model wi:
The constrained model reported in TableIIassumes that the nonzero skill group's ability is normally distributed. As such, some of these analysts can have negative true ability, that is,ωi<0. It is possible that some analysts do make recommendations that underperform on average because the analyst makes bad recommendations relative to market performance or agency problems induce the analyst to make unprofitable recommendations (e.g., Michaely and Womack (1999), Agrawal and Chen (2008)). While the constrained model estimates a fairly small number of analysts with negative ability (5.02%), this assumption may seem unreasonable to some readers. Accordingly, we estimate a version of the constrained model that restricts analysts to have either zero skill or strictly positive ability. To do so, we assume that nonzero skill analysts are drawn from a lognormal distribution rather than a normal distribution.
Specifically, a fraction1−π0of analysts are drawn from a lognormal component 1 with true abilityωidistributed as lognormal with corresponding normal distribution parametersμωandσω. We denote this lognormal density functiong. Thus, the expected ability of a skilled analyst isμ1=exp(μω+0.5σω2), and the variance of ability across skilled analysts isσ12=exp(2μω+σω2)[exp(σω2)−1]. The remaining fraction π0of analysts have true abilityωiof 0. As in the main specifications, the estimated abnormal performance is measured with estimation error,ei, which is assumed to be normally distributed with mean zero and independent ofωi. We assume that the standard deviation ofeiis equal to the standard errorsiof analysti's abnormal performance. Thus, the estimated abnormal performance isα̂i=ωi+ei.
Estimates from this alternative parameterization of the constrained mixture distribution are reported in TableAI. The estimated fraction of skilled analysts is 88.4%, which is remarkably close to the estimate from the constrained mixture model presented in TableIIof 89.4%. Thus, under the constraint that some fraction of analysts have zero skill, the parametric assumption that nonzero skill is distributed normal versus lognormal does not meaningfully affect inferences regarding the fraction of skilled analysts. The BICs in TablesII,III, andAIindicate that the constrained model with lognormal skill fits the data better than the constrained model with a normally distributed Component 1, but that the unconstrained mixture of two normal distributions (presented in TableIII) better characterizes the skill distribution than both of the constrained models. As such, the paper uses the unconstrained two-normal-component mixture model as the main specification.
